did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically pre-trained Inception-ResNetV2 models, for melanoma classification. The study trained and evaluated these deep learning models on clinical images, dermoscopy images, and paired clinical/dermoscopy images to distinguish melanoma from non-melanoma lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental results. It details a study design involving the division of 914 image pairs into training, validation, and test sets, the construction and training of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using pre-trained Inception-ResNetV2 models, and the statistical comparison of their performance metrics (AUC-ROC, sensitivity, specificity) on melanoma classification.

title: Comprehensive analysis of clinical images contributions for melanoma classification using convolutional neural networks
brief_summary: This study evaluated the efficacy of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for melanoma classification using clinical images, dermoscopy images, and paired clinical/dermoscopy images. Utilizing 914 image pairs from the 7-point criteria database and the ISIC repository, the researchers trained pre-trained Inception-ResNetV2 models with random hyperparameters. The models were assessed on their ability to distinguish primary melanoma from non-melanoma lesions using AUC-ROC, sensitivity, and specificity metrics on a held-out test set. The dermoscopy-only models achieved the highest performance with an AUC-ROC of 0.869, significantly outperforming clinical-only models (AUC-ROC 0.661). Although paired clinical and dermoscopy models showed high sensitivity (1.000), they exhibited significantly lower specificity compared to dermoscopy-only models. The findings suggest that while dermoscopy images are superior to clinical images for this task, the added value of incorporating paired clinical images remains unclear.

lesion_types_studied.details: Binary category '1' denoting any type of primary melanoma. Metastatic melanoma images were excluded. Specific subtypes included in the dataset counts were: Melanoma (0.76–1.5 mm), Melanoma (in situ), Melanoma (less than 0.76 mm), and Melanoma (more than 1.5 mm).

Binary category '0' for non-melanoma lesions, specifically nevi. The dataset had predominantly non-melanoma lesions (class imbalance). Specific subtypes included in the dataset counts were: Combined nevus, Congenital nevus, Dermal nevus, Nevus, Recurrent nevus, and Reed or spitz nevus.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of melanoma vs. non-melanoma (nevi)
all_task_types.details: The study performs binary classification to distinguish primary melanoma (class '1') from non-melanoma lesions/nevi (class '0'). The models use a trainable sigmoid unit for binary classification. Performance was evaluated using AUC-ROC, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: 7-point criteria evaluation database (SEVEN)
datasets_used.description: A public dermatology image repository providing paired clinical and dermoscopy photographs from the same pigmented skin lesion, including patient demographic characteristics, lesion localization, and diagnosis. Images were gathered from this source along with the ISIC repository.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training CNN models (clinical, dermoscopy, and paired inputs).
use.validation: Used for validation set to compare model performance metrics across hyperparameter sets.
use.testing: Used for test set to compare performance metrics of the best models.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) repository
datasets_used.description: A public dermatology image repository providing paired clinical and dermoscopy photographs from the same pigmented skin lesion, including patient demographic characteristics, lesion localization, and diagnosis. Images were gathered from this source along with the 7-point criteria evaluation database.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training CNN models (clinical, dermoscopy, and paired inputs).
use.validation: Used for validation set to compare model performance metrics across hyperparameter sets.
use.testing: Used for test set to compare performance metrics of the best models.

datasets_used.name: Study Cohort (Combined)
datasets_used.description: The total dataset used in the study, comprising 914 pairs of clinical and dermoscopy images from the SEVEN and ISIC repositories. Metastatic melanoma images were excluded. The dataset was divided into training, validation, and test sets using stratified data splitting based on repository, sex, lesion location, and definitive diagnosis.

use.training: 676 pairs used to train 60 models (20 per model type: clinical, dermoscopy, paired) using random hyperparameters.
use.validation: 119 pairs used for initial analysis and to select the best models based on validation AUC-ROC.
use.testing: 119 pairs used to compare the performance metrics (AUC-ROC, sensitivity, specificity) of the best models selected from the validation set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance metrics of the three types of models (clinical, dermoscopy, and clinical + dermoscopy) on the validation set.
To conduct pairwise comparisons between models to pinpoint specific differences when Friedman’s test revealed statistical significance.
To compare correlated ROC curves (AUC-ROC) on the test set for the best models selected from the validation set.
To compare sensitivity and specificity (non-independent samples) on the test set for the best models selected from the validation set.
To adjust p-values for multiple comparisons when making pairwise comparisons.
To determine statistical significance for reported p-values.

hypothesis_testing.test: Friedman’s test, Wilcoxon signed-ranks tests, De-Long test, McNemar’s test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in performance metrics (AUC-ROC, sensitivity, specificity) between the compared model types (clinical vs. dermoscopy, clinical vs. clinical+dermoscopy, dermoscopy vs. clinical+dermoscopy).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in performance metrics between the compared model types.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Significance was determined using p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons via the Bonferroni method. Significant differences were found in AUC-ROC between clinical and dermoscopy models (p < 0.001) and clinical and clinical+dermoscopy models (p = 0.001). Significant differences were found in sensitivity between clinical and dermoscopy models (p = 0.01), dermoscopy and clinical+dermoscopy models (p = 0.02), and clinical and clinical+dermoscopy models (p < 0.001). Significant differences were found in specificity between dermoscopy and clinical+dermoscopy models (p < 0.001) and clinical and clinical+dermoscopy models (p < 0.001). No significant difference in AUC-ROC was found between dermoscopy and clinical+dermoscopy models.

Study Cohort (Combined).description: 914 image pairs from SEVEN and ISIC repositories, divided into training (676), validation (119), and test (119) sets.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Convolutional layers with ImageNet-trained weights preserved for feature extraction
architecture_details.single_image_models: Global average pooling followed by variable dropout probability
architecture_details.paired_image_models: Two Inception-ResNetV2 architectures (one for clinical, one for dermoscopy) with concatenated outputs after global average pooling

training_details.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy with weight adjustment for class imbalance
training_details.hyperparameters: Randomly generated sets of learning rate, batch size, epochs, and dropout probability (20 sets)
training_details.training_environment: Python 3, Google Colab virtual machine with 83.5GB RAM and A100 Nvidia GPU
training_details.model_variants: 60 models total (20 per type: clinical, dermoscopy, clinical+dermoscopy)

classification_or_output_head.structure: Replaced final softmax layer, applied after global average pooling and dropout

preprocessing_techniques: Border removal: Rows with maximum color intensity below 100 identified as black background and removed; process repeated four times by rotating the image 90 degrees in each iteration.
Segmentation masking: Pigmented lesion highlighted as an 'active' region by applying a mask using thresholding.
Cropping: Image cropped based on non-active pixels from the segmentation mask.
Standard preprocessing: Applied standard preprocessing steps used in the original InceptionResNetV2 CNN architecture (available in TensorFlow).
conclusions: CNN models trained on dermoscopy images significantly outperformed those relying solely on clinical images for melanoma classification (AUC-ROC 0.869 vs. 0.661, p < 0.001).

The potential advantages of incorporating paired clinical and dermoscopy images for CNN-based melanoma classification appear less clear, as they did not significantly improve AUC-ROC compared to dermoscopy-only models.
While clinical + dermoscopy models achieved the highest sensitivity (1.000), they exhibited significantly lower specificity (0.288) compared to dermoscopy-only models (0.800, p < 0.001).
Dermoscopy models demonstrated significantly less overfitting (smaller difference in AUC-ROC between training and validation sets) compared to clinical and clinical + dermoscopy models.
Clinical images alone provided inferior diagnostic performance compared to dermoscopy images, confirming the superior efficacy of dermoscopy for this task under the study conditions.
important_remarks: The study utilized a relatively small sample size (914 image pairs) for deep learning, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation, though they mitigated this via transfer learning.
The dataset lacked information on patient ancestry or skin phototype, limiting the generalizability of the results to diverse skin types.
The dataset exhibited class imbalance with predominantly non-melanoma lesions (nevi), although a weighted loss function was used to address prediction bias.
Metastatic melanoma images were excluded, meaning the models were trained exclusively on primary melanoma lesions.
The study did not provide details regarding the technical specifications of the imaging systems used for the clinical and dermoscopy images.

The clinical + dermoscopy models achieved perfect sensitivity (1.000) but significantly lower specificity (0.288) compared to dermoscopy-only models (0.800), indicating a trade-off in diagnostic performance.
Dermoscopy models demonstrated significantly less overfitting (smaller difference in AUC-ROC between training and validation sets) compared to clinical and clinical + dermoscopy models.

The potential advantages of incorporating paired clinical and dermoscopy images for CNN-based melanoma classification were found to be unclear, as they did not significantly improve AUC-ROC compared to dermoscopy-only models.
The study relied on public repositories (SEVEN and ISIC) which may introduce selection bias, as image selection was based on availability of paired images and diagnosis.
